topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. It was a like from Mr. Harrison. My stomach clenched, a familiar knot tightening in my chest. I stared at the screen, the digital representation of his casual engagement feeling like a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders. Every scroll through his recent posts felt like an accusation. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling slightly as I reached for a glass of water. The thought of him seeing my latest, poorly composed selfie—the one I thought was just a harmless snapshot—made my breath catch. I spent the next hour meticulously re-reading every comment he’d left on a public post, searching for some hidden malice, any sign that this was more than just an accidental follow. The air in my room felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

It started innocently enough—a shared class group, a necessary exchange of academic resources. But then, the pattern became undeniable. A post about a difficult assignment I’d struggled with was suddenly liked, then commented on by him. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I kept glancing at my notifications, a nervous habit that had become compulsive. When I finally dared to check his profile directly, the silence of his curated feed was deafening. There, there was a picture of a landscape, and underneath it, a caption that was pointedly, almost deliberately, aimed at the subject of my current essay. A wave of hot flush spread across my neck. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing loudly in the small apartment. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my pulse.

[story 3]

I found myself monitoring the activity on his account during breaks, a secret, obsessive ritual. It wasn't about the content of his posts; it was the knowledge that he was *there*, observing. When I saw a story he’d posted—a brief video clip of him laughing with colleagues—a sharp, unpleasant sensation shot through me. My hands started shaking uncontrollably as I tried to focus on my textbook, but the words swam before my eyes. I felt a deep, internal churning, like acid was working its way through my insides. I pulled my hoodie tighter around myself, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. Every time I looked up, the possibility of him knowing I was watching, that I was being cataloged, made my skin crawl with a profound sense of exposure.
